questions.explanation: Composite supply occurs when a commodity can be supplied from different sources or produced by different firms to satisfy the same want. In a monopolistic market, different firms supply beverages (different brands of soft drinks) to meet the same consumer demand for drinks. Competitive supply (B) refers to alternative products that can be produced with the same resources.

questions.question: If the government fixes the price of a commodity below the equilibrium price, this will lead to:
questions.explanation: A price ceiling (maximum price) set below the equilibrium price makes the good artificially cheap. At this low price, the quantity demanded by consumers will be greater than the quantity supplied by producers. This shortage is called excess demand. In a free market, this would put upward pressure on the price, but the fixed price prevents the market from clearing.

questions.question: One criticism of the price mechanism (market system) is that it:
options: provides a low degree of freedom
widens the gap between rich and poor (inequality)
questions.explanation: A major criticism of the price mechanism is that it can lead to an unequal distribution of income and widen the gap between rich and poor. It rewards those who already own resources (capital, land) and may not provide for those unable to work or lacking resources. This can result in significant economic inequality without government intervention.

questions.question: To make housing more affordable for low-income earners, the government may impose rent control by fixing rent:
questions.explanation: To make accommodation more affordable (reduce cost for renters), the government can impose a maximum price or rent control. This price ceiling must be set below the market equilibrium price to be effective. Setting it below equilibrium makes rent cheaper for those who can find housing, though it may create a shortage.
questions.hint: Rent control = price ceiling = below equilibrium.

questions.question: If a refinery reduces its average cost by purchasing crude oil in bulk, it is enjoying:
questions.explanation: Economies of scale refer to the cost advantages a firm enjoys as it increases its scale of production. Buying inputs like crude oil in large quantities often leads to bulk discounts, which lower the average cost per unit. This is a specific type of economies of scale known as purchasing economies.
questions.hint: Bulk buying = lower average cost = economies of scale.

questions.question: An isoquant that lies farther from the origin represents:
questions.explanation: An isoquant shows all combinations of inputs (e.g., labour and capital) that produce the same level of output. An isoquant that is farther from the origin (above and to the right) represents a higher level of output because it requires more inputs to produce that larger quantity.
questions.hint: Further from origin = more inputs = more output.

questions.question: In a typical cost curve diagram, the vertical distance between the Total Cost (TC) curve and the Total Variable Cost (TVC) curve represents:
questions.explanation: Total Cost (TC) = Total Fixed Cost (TFC) + Total Variable Cost (TVC). Since TFC is constant, the vertical distance between the TC curve and the TVC curve at any output level represents the Total Fixed Cost (TFC). The two curves are parallel because fixed cost is constant.

questions.question: The profit-maximizing output for a firm in any market structure is where:
options: marginal revenue equals marginal cost (MR = MC)
questions.explanation: The profit-maximizing condition for any firm, regardless of market structure (perfect competition, monopoly, etc.), is where Marginal Revenue (MR) equals Marginal Cost (MC). Producing beyond this point would add more to cost than to revenue, reducing profit. Producing less would forego profitable opportunities.

questions.question: Total Revenue (TR) for a firm is calculated as:
questions.explanation: Total Revenue (TR) is the total amount of money a firm receives from selling its output. It is calculated by multiplying the price per unit (P) by the quantity sold (Q). The formula is TR = P × Q.

questions.question: The measure of national income most commonly used to compare the standard of living among nations is:
questions.explanation: To compare the average standard of living or economic well-being of citizens across different countries, we use per capita income (or per capita GDP). This is calculated by dividing the country's total national income by its total population. It gives an average income per person, allowing for more meaningful comparisons than total income, which is influenced by population size.
questions.hint: Per capita = per person = for comparison across countries.
questions.formula: Per Capita Income = National Income / Population

questions.question: According to Keynesian theory, the speculative demand for money is inversely related to the:
questions.explanation: The speculative demand for money is the desire to hold cash as an asset to take advantage of future changes in bond prices. When interest rates are high, people expect them to fall (bond prices to rise), so they buy bonds and hold less money. When interest rates are low, they hold more money, waiting for rates to rise. Thus, speculative demand is inversely related to the interest rate.
questions.hint: High interest → hold less speculative money.

questions.question: If Mr. K obtains a ₦50,000 loan from a bank to pay for household expenses (food, rent, utilities), this is an example of:
questions.explanation: The transactions demand for money is the need for cash to carry out everyday purchases and pay for regular expenses like household needs. A loan taken specifically to cover such day-to-day spending is primarily for transactionary purposes. It is not for speculation (betting on asset prices) or precaution (emergency savings).

questions.question: Which of the following is a monetary policy tool used by the Central Bank of Nigeria to control inflation?
questions.explanation: The Central Bank's primary tool for controlling inflation is monetary policy, particularly the discount rate (also called Monetary Policy Rate or MPR). Raising the discount rate makes borrowing more expensive for commercial banks, which increases lending rates, reduces money supply, and helps curb inflation. Tax rates and government expenditure are fiscal policy tools used by the government.

questions.question: If the Central Bank reduces the money supply, the interest rate will:
questions.explanation: Interest rate is the 'price' of borrowing money. If the Central Bank reduces the money supply, there is less money available for lending. According to the law of supply and demand, when the supply of loanable funds decreases (with demand constant), the price (interest rate) will rise. Higher interest rates discourage borrowing and spending, helping to control inflation.
questions.hint: Less money supply → higher interest rates.

questions.question: An example of an expansionary fiscal policy action is:
options: a decrease in corporate profit tax rates
questions.explanation: Expansionary fiscal policy aims to stimulate economic growth by increasing aggregate demand. This can be done by increasing government spending or cutting taxes. A decrease in corporate profit tax rates leaves businesses with more after-tax profit, encouraging investment and expansion. The other options (decreasing spending, increasing taxes, cutting welfare) are contractionary.
questions.hint: Expansionary = tax cuts or spending increases.

questions.question: A tax on land (which is in fixed supply) will ultimately fall:
questions.explanation: Land is in fixed supply; its quantity does not change with price (perfectly inelastic supply). Therefore, a tax on land cannot be passed on to users (tenants) in the form of higher rent because the landlord cannot reduce the supply of land. The tax burden falls entirely on the owner of the land in the form of reduced rental income.
questions.hint: Fixed supply → tax borne by owner.

questions.question: One of the goals of development plans in Nigeria is to:
options: increase the profitability of multinational businesses
achieve a higher standard of living for citizens
questions.explanation: The ultimate goal of any national development plan is to improve the welfare of the citizens, encapsulated in achieving a higher standard of living. This involves not just economic growth (increasing GDP) but also better healthcare, education, infrastructure, and reduced poverty. It is a broader, people-centered objective.
questions.hint: Development plans aim to improve people's lives.

questions.question: Life insurance companies contribute to economic development by investing a significant portion of their assets in:
questions.explanation: Life insurance companies collect premiums over long periods and hold large pools of long-term funds. They contribute to economic development by investing these funds in long-term financial instruments like corporate bonds, stocks, and mortgages for housing and industrial projects. This provides capital for businesses and infrastructure, fostering growth.
questions.hint: Life insurance = long-term funds → long-term investments.

questions.question: To add value to Nigeria's agricultural produce, it is necessary to:
options: process raw materials into finished goods
advertise products in European markets only
questions.explanation: Adding value means increasing the economic worth of a product. For agricultural produce, this often involves processing raw materials into finished or semi-finished goods. For example, turning cocoa beans into chocolate, cassava into starch or ethanol, or palm fruit into palm oil. Processing creates jobs, increases export earnings, and reduces post-harvest losses.

questions.question: A major reason for low agricultural productivity in West Africa is the:
options: predominance of subsistence farming using crude implements
questions.explanation: Low agricultural productivity in West Africa is attributed to many factors, but a primary reason is the predominance of subsistence farming using crude implements (hoes, cutlasses) instead of modern machinery and technology. This limits the scale, efficiency, and output of farming. Other factors include poor infrastructure, lack of credit, and reliance on rainfall.

questions.question: The most important determinant for the location of a brick industry is the availability of:
questions.explanation: Brick-making uses clay as its primary raw material. Clay is heavy and bulky relative to its value. To minimize transport costs, the brick industry is best located near the source of the raw material (clay deposits). This is a classic example of a material-oriented industry.
questions.hint: Clay is heavy → locate near source.

questions.question: In Nigeria, efficiency in public corporations can be improved through:
questions.explanation: Public corporations in Nigeria have often been criticized for inefficiency, corruption, and losses. Privatization—transferring ownership and control to the private sector—is advocated as a way to introduce competition, profit motive, and better management, thereby improving efficiency and productivity. Nationalization (government takeover) is the opposite and not typically associated with increased efficiency.

questions.question: Government participation in the Nigerian oil industry was necessitated by:
options: the formation of OPEC and the desire for sovereignty over resources
high demand for crude oil only
questions.explanation: The formation of the Organization of Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC) in 1960 encouraged member countries to take greater control of their oil resources. In Nigeria, this led to the establishment of the Nigerian National Oil Corporation (NNOC) in 1971 and later the NNPC in 1977, marking significant government participation to assert sovereignty and maximize benefits from the industry.

questions.question: The deregulation of the petroleum sector in Nigeria is expected to bring about:
options: an end to foreign firms' dominance
questions.explanation: Deregulation involves removing government controls, particularly on the pricing and distribution of petroleum products. The expected outcome is to allow market forces to determine prices, which should attract private investment in refineries and distribution, leading to greater efficiency, increased competition, and potentially more stable supply.

questions.question: A distinguishing characteristic of a consumer co-operative society is that:
options: the maximum number of shareholders is 20
the minimum number of shareholders is 5
questions.explanation: A consumer co-operative society is owned and controlled by its members, who are the consumers (not the workers). The members pool resources to buy goods in bulk and sell to themselves at reduced prices, sharing any profits (surplus) among themselves. The key principle is that the members are the owners.

questions.question: A major disadvantage of a partnership business is:
questions.explanation: A partnership is a business owned by two or more people. A major disadvantage is unlimited liability—each partner is personally liable for all business debts, not just their share. This means creditors can seize partners' personal assets (houses, cars) if the business cannot pay its debts. Potential disagreements among partners are also a disadvantage.

questions.question: The quality of the labour force in Nigeria can be improved by:
options: encouraging the study of science and technology only
establishing more skills acquisition and vocational training centres
questions.explanation: The quality of the labour force refers to the skills, education, and health of workers. While universities are important, a direct way to improve practical skills, especially for those not in formal education, is through vocational training and skills acquisition centres. These equip people with employable skills like carpentry, welding, and ICT, directly enhancing labour quality and productivity.

questions.question: The effect of emigration on a country's population is to:
questions.explanation: Emigration is the movement of people out of a country to settle in another. This directly reduces the country's population. It can also lead to a 'brain drain' if skilled workers leave. It may decrease job competition (not decrease job opportunities) for those who remain, but the primary demographic effect is a decrease in population size.

questions.question: To prevent the external value of the naira from falling (depreciating), the Central Bank can:
options: increase its spending of foreign reserves
buy its own currency (naira) using foreign reserves
questions.explanation: To support the naira's value (prevent depreciation), the Central Bank can intervene in the foreign exchange market by selling foreign reserves and buying naira. This increases demand for the naira (pushing its value up) and reduces its supply. Selling naira (B) would increase supply and cause it to fall further.
questions.hint: To support currency: buy it with foreign reserves.

questions.question: A fiscal policy instrument that can influence the pattern of demand in an economy is:
questions.explanation: Fiscal policy involves government spending and taxation. Income tax directly affects consumers' disposable income. By increasing or decreasing income tax rates, the government can influence how much people have to spend, thereby affecting the demand pattern for goods and services. For example, a tax cut increases disposable income, boosting demand for consumer goods.
questions.hint: Income tax changes → disposable income changes → demand pattern changes.

questions.question: One of the main achievements of the Economic Commission for Africa (ECA) is:
options: eliminating all trade restrictions among African states
encouraging regional integration and reducing trade barriers
guaranteeing a steady flow of foreign investment into Africa
providing a single currency for Africa
questions.explanation: The UN Economic Commission for Africa (ECA) promotes economic and social development. One of its key roles is fostering regional integration. It has been instrumental in facilitating agreements and frameworks that aim to reduce trade restrictions among African states, such as working towards the African Continental Free Trade Area (AfCFTA).

questions.question: The rate of interest charged on a loan depends largely on:
options: the risk associated with the loan
questions.explanation: Lenders charge interest to compensate for the risk of not being repaid, the time value of money, and inflation. The risk associated with the loan (default risk) is a primary factor. A higher-risk borrower (e.g., no collateral, unstable income) will be charged a higher interest rate to compensate the lender for the greater chance of default.

questions.question: Real wages grow sustainably when there is:
options: an increase in the rate of labour productivity
a rising cost of capital accumulation
a contraction of employment in service industries
an increase in the quantity of labour
questions.explanation: Real wages (wages adjusted for inflation) grow when workers produce more per hour—that is, when labour productivity increases. If a worker's output per hour rises, the value they create for the firm increases, allowing for higher wages without causing inflation. Increases in the quantity of labour may lower wages due to increased supply. Productivity growth is the sustainable driver of real wage growth.

questions.question: If Mr. X lost his clerical job and searched for a similar job for ten months before finding one, this is an example of:
questions.explanation: Frictional unemployment is short-term unemployment that occurs when people are between jobs, searching for new employment that matches their skills. It is a natural part of a dynamic economy. Mr. X lost a clerical job and searched for a similar one for ten months. This period of job search, despite his skills being in demand, represents frictional unemployment.
